This study reveals that ferroptosis—iron-dependent cell death driven by lipid peroxidation—is mechanistically involved in adenomyosis pathogenesis through a novel METTL3-m6A-YTHDF1-GPX4 regulatory axis. METTL3, a methyltransferase catalyzing m6A RNA modification, is significantly downregulated in adenomyotic myometrial lesions, resulting in reduced m6A methylation on GPX4 mRNA and decreased YTHDF1-mediated GPX4 translation. Consequently, lowered GPX4 protein—a critical ferroptosis suppressor that prevents toxic lipid peroxidation—leaves endometrial cells vulnerable to iron-dependent ferroptosis, characterized by elevated intracellular ferrous iron, reactive oxygen species, and malondialdehyde accumulation. GPX4 expression inversely correlates with adenomyosis severity markers including CA125 levels, uterine size, and dysmenorrhea severity. Experimental evidence demonstrates that ferroptosis inhibition via ferrostatin-1 ameliorates adenomyosis pathology, whereas ferroptosis induction via erastin or METTL3 inhibition exacerbates disease, establishing ferroptosis as a potential therapeutic target in adenomyosis.

title: Erastin-induced multi-pathway cell death in endometriosis: a mechanistic and translational narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Erastin induces ferroptosis in endometriotic lesions by inhibiting the system Xc− transporter, which depletes intracellular glutathione (GSH) and inactivates the antioxidant enzyme GPX4, ultimately leading to accumulation of lipid peroxides and membrane rupture in ectopic endometrial stromal cells. In endometriosis, iron overload—resulting from retrograde menstruation and hemoglobin breakdown by peritoneal macrophages—drives ferroptosis through iron-catalyzed reactive oxygen species (ROS) production via the Fenton reaction, which promotes lipid peroxidation and further activates the NF-κB pathway to upregulate matrix metalloproteinases and exacerbate inflammation. Notably, ferroptosis in ectopic endometrial stromal cells paradoxically promotes secretion of angiogenic factors like VEGFA and IL-8, which support neovascularization and lesion progression despite cell death. Some ectopic endometrial stromal cells exhibit ferroptosis resistance through the ATF4–xCT pathway and Fibulin-1 expression, enabling survival in iron-rich environments. Current therapeutic approaches, including progestins like dienogest and newer GnRH antagonists (elagolix, linzagolix, relugolix), aim to treat endometriosis through hormonal suppression, though ferroptosis-based mechanisms may offer complementary therapeutic targets for managing this complex gynecological disorder affecting 6–10% of women of reproductive age.

title: Multiple oestradiol functions inhibit ferroptosis and acute kidney injury
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that estradiol and its hydroxylated derivatives protect kidney tubules from ferroptosis-mediated acute kidney injury through multiple complementary mechanisms, explaining why females are naturally resistant to acute kidney injury compared to males. Ferroptosis, an iron-catalyzed form of cell death, drives acute tubular necrosis and subsequent nephron loss during kidney ischemia-reperfusion injury. Estradiol functions through both non-genomic pathways, where 2-hydroxyoestradiol and 4-hydroxyoestradiol act as radical-trapping antioxidants and iron chelators to directly inhibit lipid peroxidation, and genomic pathways via estrogen receptor 1 (ESR1) that upregulate the anti-ferroptotic hydropersulfide system (particularly glutathione hydropersulfide) while suppressing pro-ferroptotic ether lipid pathway proteins. FSP1 protein regenerates oxidized estradiol derivatives, amplifying their antioxidant activity and maintaining ferroptosis resistance. Female kidney tubules naturally accumulate high levels of protective hydroxyoestradiol metabolites and express elevated levels of the hydropersulfide-generating enzyme CSE, whereas male tubules express pro-ferroptotic ether lipid pathway proteins and elevated ETHE1, which degrades protective hydropersulfides. Ovariectomized females and ESR1-deficient female mice lose ferroptosis resistance and become susceptible to acute kidney injury, demonstrating that estrogen-mediated mechanisms are essential for the sex-based protection observed clinically.

title: The Mechanism of Ferroptosis Regulating Granulosa Cell Apoptosis and Oxidative Stress Through the NF-κB/PTGS2 Axis in Porcine Atretic Follicles
summary:
# Summary

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death involving lipid peroxidation and glutathione depletion, drives granulosa cell apoptosis and follicular atresia in porcine ovaries through activation of the NF-κB/PTGS2 signaling axis. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that ferroptosis markers and related genes are significantly upregulated in atretic follicles compared to healthy follicles, with PTGS2 differentially expressed between these states. NF-κB nuclear translocation regulates PTGS2 gene expression, which amplifies ferroptosis-induced granulosa cell death and subsequent follicular atresia. Melatonin, a neurohormone synthesized locally by oocytes, granulosa cells, and cumulus cells within follicular fluid, protects against ferroptosis by inhibiting the NF-κB/PTGS2 axis and downregulating ferroptosis marker genes. This mechanism highlights melatonin's potential therapeutic role in preventing oxidative stress-induced follicular loss and maintaining ovarian function through regulation of iron-dependent cell death pathways.

title: Natural Compounds as Modulators of Ferroptosis: Mechanistic Insights and Therapeutic Prospects in Breast Cancer
summary:
# Article Summary

This review examines ferroptosis, an iron-dependent regulated cell death mechanism characterized by iron accumulation and lipid peroxidation, as an emerging therapeutic strategy for breast cancer resistant to conventional treatments. Ferroptosis operates through multiple interconnected pathways: iron homeostasis dysregulation via ferritinophagy (controlled by NCOA4 and ATM kinase), lipid peroxidation involving ACSL4-activated polyunsaturated fatty acids, and antioxidant defense systems including the GPX4/GSH axis, FSP1/CoQ10 pathway, and NRF2-mediated transcription. Natural products such as curcumin, resveratrol, and oridonin have emerged as potent ferroptosis inducers, with oridonin specifically enhancing sensitivity in triple-negative breast cancer through NCOA4-mediated ferritinophagy. Different breast cancer subtypes exhibit variable ferroptosis susceptibility: triple-negative cancers show inherent sensitivity due to epithelial-mesenchymal transition-driven PUFA integration, while hormone-receptor-positive luminal tumors display ferroptosis resistance that can be overcome by CDK4/6 inhibitors suppressing SLC7A11. Additionally, ferroptosis triggers immunogenic cell death through damage-associated molecular pattern release, activating dendritic cells and CD8+ T lymphocyte infiltration, thereby reshaping the anti-tumor immune microenvironment.

title: Thymol Preserves Spermatogenesis and Androgen Production in Cisplatin-Induced Testicular Toxicity by Modulating Ferritinophagy, Oxidative Stress, and the Keap1/Nrf2/HO-1 Pathway
summary:
# Summary

Cisplatin (CDDP), a widely used chemotherapy agent, causes testicular toxicity and reproductive failure through mechanisms involving iron dysregulation, oxidative stress, and ferroptosis. In this study, thymol, a natural monoterpene phenol from Thymus vulgaris, protected against CDDP-induced gonadotoxicity by suppressing ferritinophagy—the selective autophagy of iron-storage ferritin—thereby reducing iron overload and subsequent reactive oxygen species generation via Fenton chemistry. Thymol simultaneously activated the Keap1/Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant pathway, which enhances cellular defenses, while downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6. These protective mechanisms preserved spermatogenesis, sperm quality, testicular architecture, and steroidogenesis in CDDP-treated animals. The findings suggest that thymol co-administration may mitigate chemotherapy-induced reproductive toxicity while potentially enhancing CDDP's selectivity toward cancer cells over normal tissues.

title: Quinazolinone derivatives as potential anti-tumor agents: Structural features and molecular mechanisms in inducing cell death (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Quinazolinone derivatives represent a promising class of compounds that induce multiple forms of cell death through distinct molecular mechanisms. These agents trigger apoptosis via intrinsic mitochondrial pathways by modulating the PI3K-AKT and MAPK signaling cascades, which culminate in Bcl-2 family protein rebalancing, cytochrome C release, and caspase-9-mediated activation of executioner caspases. Additionally, quinazolinone compounds induce ferroptosis—an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction—through mechanisms including GPX4 inhibition, GSH depletion, and ROS accumulation. Specific derivatives such as Erastin and CQ-Mito promote ferroptosis by targeting ferroptosis-regulating proteins, while compounds like Mdivi-1 paradoxically inhibit ferroptosis by suppressing mitochondrial fission and restoring antioxidant defenses. Quinazolinone compounds also induce autophagy as an alternative cell death pathway, and several derivatives modulate tubulin dynamics and endoplasmic reticulum stress. These multi-targeted mechanisms, particularly the dual induction of apoptosis and ferroptosis alongside oxidative stress intensification, position quinazolinone derivatives as potent anti-tumor agents with relevance to conditions involving dysregulated cell death pathways.

title: SAT1 promotes the progression of OA by regulating TRIM33-mediated p53 acetylation to enhance ferroptosis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies SAT1 as a critical promoter of osteoarthritis progression through a novel regulatory axis involving ferroptosis. SAT1, a key polyamine metabolism enzyme, promotes the SAT1-TRIM33-p53 signaling pathway by downregulating TRIM33, which normally inhibits p53 acetylation; increased p53 acetylation then enhances ferroptosis in chondrocytes. Ferroptosis is characterized by iron accumulation, glutathione depletion, mitochondrial dysfunction, and lipid peroxidation, all of which contribute to chondrocyte loss and OA pathogenesis. Experimental evidence demonstrates that SAT1 overexpression induces ferroptosis through increased lipid reactive oxygen species, intracellular iron, and ACSL4 expression while decreasing glutathione and GPX4 levels, effects reversible by ferroptosis inhibitors and SAT1 knockdown. The findings suggest that SAT1 inhibition represents a potential therapeutic strategy for osteoarthritis by suppressing ferroptosis-mediated chondrocyte damage and inflammation.

title: Targeting ferroptosis to rescue osteogenic differentiation in BRONJ-affected jawbone mesenchymal stem cells: the role of miR-145-3p and exosome-mediated therapy
summary:
# Summary

Bisphosphonate-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (BRONJ) is driven by ferroptosis, a form of iron-dependent cell death that compromises mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) viability and suppresses osteogenic differentiation by reducing alkaline phosphatase activity and expression of osteogenic markers RUNX2 and OCN. The study identifies a critical miR-145-3p/IREB2 regulatory circuit that governs ferroptosis in BRONJ-derived MSCs, with circulating miR-145 levels reduced in BRONJ-affected animals. Exosome-mediated delivery of miR-145-3p mitigates ferroptosis and reinstates osteogenic competence in jawbone-derived MSCs, both restoring local bone regeneration and reinstating systemic osteogenic potential. When engineered into cell-sheet/hydroxyapatite-TCP scaffold constructs or administered systemically, these exosomal miR-145-3p-treated MSCs achieve robust repair of critical-sized bone defects and significantly elevate bone mass in osteoporotic animal models. These findings establish exosomal miR-145-3p as a therapeutic strategy to ameliorate both localized and systemic complications of BRONJ by targeting the ferroptosis pathway, highlighting the broader interplay between iron homeostasis, ferroptosis, and bone metabolism.

title: Ferroptosis-mediated osteoclast-osteoblast crosstalk: signaling pathways governing bone remodeling in osteoporosis
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic Surgery and Research
summary:
# Summary

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death driven by lipid peroxidation, emerges as a key mechanistic driver of osteoporosis through disruption of bone remodeling homeostasis. Iron overload promotes reactive oxygen species (ROS) production via the Fenton reaction and inactivates glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), the central ferroptosis inhibitor that normally reduces lipid peroxides through glutathione-dependent catalytic activity. Dysregulation of iron homeostasis—involving increased transferrin receptor 1 (TFR1)-mediated iron uptake and suppressed ferroportin-mediated iron export due to ROS-induced oxidative damage—perpetuates ferroptosis in both osteoclasts and osteoblasts, compromising bone formation while promoting bone matrix degradation and inflammatory responses. Multiple anti-ferroptotic pathways, including the NRF2-GPX4 axis, FSP1-CoQ10 antioxidant system, system Xc⁻-mediated cystine uptake, and heat shock proteins, converge to protect bone cells from ferroptotic death. The therapeutic potential of ferroptosis inhibition in osteoporosis is underscored by the identification of key regulatory nodes—such as SLC7A11, NRF2, TRPV1, and ferritinophagy—that could be targeted to restore bone remodeling balance and prevent pathological bone loss.

title: Thymosin α1 alleviates pulpitis by inhibiting ferroptosis of dental pulp cells
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death, plays a central role in pulpitis pathogenesis through elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) and ferrous iron (Fe2+) levels coupled with reduced expression of the ferroptosis inhibitor GPX4. Thymosin α1 (Tα1), a 28-amino acid peptide derived from prothymosin α, alleviates pulpitis by suppressing ferroptosis in dental pulp cells through multiple mechanisms: upregulating the antioxidant enzyme GPX4 and iron storage protein FTL, reducing intracellular Fe2+ levels, and decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and the pro-ferroptotic factor PTGS2. The study further reveals a bidirectional relationship between ferroptosis and inflammation, where inflammatory activation can induce ferroptosis while ferroptosis-mediated release of damage-associated molecular patterns amplifies immune cell infiltration. These findings establish ferroptosis inhibition as a viable therapeutic strategy for pulpitis, with thymosin α1 offering dual immunomodulatory and ferroptosis-suppressive benefits that promote dental pulp cell survival and tissue homeostasis.

title: Targeting metalloptosis in tumor therapy: from molecular mechanisms to application of metal nanoparticles
summary:
# Article Summary

This article examines metalloptosis—cell death triggered by metal ion dysregulation—as a novel therapeutic strategy for cancer treatment. The review identifies multiple metal-dependent regulated cell death pathways, including ferroptosis (Fe²⁺-dependent), cuproptosis (Cu²⁺-dependent), lysozincrosis (Zn²⁺-dependent), calcicoptosis (Ca²⁺-dependent), and NECSO (Na⁺-dependent), each offering distinct mechanisms for tumor suppression. Ferroptosis, the most extensively studied pathway, involves lipid peroxidation triggered by iron accumulation and is tightly regulated by the NRF2 transcription factor, which upregulates protective genes including GPX4, GSH synthesis enzymes, and the cystine transporter SLC7A11 to maintain cellular iron homeostasis. The article highlights how cancer cells exploit ferroptosis resistance through NRF2 hyperactivation and P53-mediated suppression of cystine uptake, making non-apoptotic cell death pathways attractive targets for overcoming chemoresistance. Metal nanoparticles exploiting ferroptosis and cuproptosis represent emerging nanotherapeutic approaches with significant potential for cancer treatment, particularly in tumors resistant to conventional apoptosis-inducing strategies.

title: Modulation of ferroptosis via YY1-SLC7A11 axis in hepatic
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies the NEDD4L-YY1-SLC7A11 axis as a critical regulator of ferroptosis in hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury (IRI). During IRI, the E3 ubiquitin ligase NEDD4L is upregulated and promotes K63-linked ubiquitination of YY1 at the K339 site, leading to proteasomal degradation of YY1 and subsequent suppression of SLC7A11 transcription, which consequently facilitates ferroptotic cell death. Conversely, YY1 overexpression activates SLC7A11 expression and potently inhibits ferroptosis while alleviating hepatic injury, as evidenced by reduced liver enzyme levels (ALT, AST) and inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, MCP1). NEDD4L knockdown similarly protects against IRI-induced ferroptosis by preventing YY1 degradation and maintaining SLC7A11 expression. These findings demonstrate that the NEDD4L/YY1 signaling axis represents a promising therapeutic target for mitigating ferroptosis-associated hepatic damage through modulation of the ferroptosis inhibitor SLC7A11.

title: Targeting ferroptosis as a therapeutic strategy for hepatotoxicity
summary: Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death distinct from apoptosis and necroptosis, emerges as a critical mechanism in multiple hepatic pathologies including drug-induced liver injury, alcoholic liver disease, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, viral hepatitis, ischemia-reperfusion injury, hepatic fibrosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. The process is governed by interconnected metabolic pathways centered on lipid peroxidation and redox homeostasis, including the glutathione-GPX4 system, FSP1-CoQ10 system, and BH4/DHFR system, with NADPH serving as a central regulatory hub that connects pentose phosphate pathway, ME1, and IDH1 metabolism to ferroptosis resistance. Multiple protective mechanisms exist, including NRF2-mediated upregulation of antioxidant response elements (FTH1, FTL1, HMOX1), MARCHF6-dependent degradation of pro-ferroptotic factors (p53, ACSL4), cystine import via system x_c⁻, and lipid remodeling through SREBP1-SCD1-mediated MUFA incorporation. Conversely, pro-ferroptotic pathways are promoted by ACSL4-LPCAT3-mediated PUFA incorporation into phospholipids, ether lipid metabolism (AGPS, TMEM164), and lipid uptake via CD36/FATP transporters, while DECR1 and TPD52 provide protection through catabolic and sequestration mechanisms respectively. Pharmacological targeting through ferroptosis inducers (statins, FIN56) or ferroptosis inhibitors offers therapeutic potential for managing hepat

title: Iron overload-induced ferroptosis in CD8+ T cells leads to functional abnormalities that promote endometriosis progression
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that iron overload in endometriotic lesions triggers ferroptosis in CD8+ T cells through a p53-dependent mechanism, ultimately impairing anti-tumor immunity and promoting disease progression. Iron accumulation from recurring lesion bleeding and shedding activates p53, which suppresses the ferroptosis-protective factors xCT and GPX4, leading to lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial damage via reactive oxygen species generation through the Fenton reaction. Although CD8+ T cells are enriched within ectopic endometrial lesions, they exhibit reduced activation and cytotoxic capacity due to ferroptosis-induced dysfunction, and their depletion significantly increases lesion burden in mouse models. Blocking ferroptosis with inhibitors or p53 suppression restores CD8+ T cell viability and function, demonstrating that this ferroptotic pathway is mechanistically central to endometriosis pathogenesis. The iron-mediated ferroptosis of CD8+ T cells creates an immunosuppressive microenvironment that fosters chronic inflammation, adhesions, and the clinical symptoms of chronic pain and infertility. These findings identify ferroptosis as a key therapeutic target, suggesting that iron chelation or ferroptosis inhibition could restore protective immunity and improve treatment outcomes in endometriosis patients.

title: A PCD-related prognostic signature and the oncogenic role of TRIM8 in osteosarcoma progression
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a programmed cell death (PCD)-based prognostic signature for osteosarcoma, an aggressive pediatric bone tumor that frequently metastasizes to the lungs. The researchers identified 285 differentially expressed genes enriched in apoptosis, autophagy, and necrosis pathways, which they integrated into a cell death index (CDI) model for survival prediction and therapeutic stratification. TRIM8 emerged as a key oncogenic driver that promotes osteosarcoma cell proliferation and migration, while the CDI model demonstrated strong predictive accuracy for drug sensitivity and immunotherapy response. Patients with low CDI values showed potential benefit from immunotherapy, as indicated by TIDE score analysis. The study highlights how multiple PCD mechanisms—including apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, ferroptosis, and parthanatos—regulate osteosarcoma progression and offer therapeutic opportunities. These findings suggest that PCD-based biomarkers can improve patient stratification and guide personalized treatment decisions in osteosarcoma management.

title: Immunometabolic regulation of disulfidptosis in orthopedic diseases: mechanistic heterogeneity and therapeutic targets
summary:
# Summary

This review examines disulfidptosis, a newly characterized form of ferroptosis-independent cell death driven by dysregulated cystine metabolism in orthopedic diseases. Disulfidptosis is triggered when cells with high SLC7A11 expression encounter glucose deprivation, which suppresses the pentose phosphate pathway and depletes NADPH, preventing the reduction of accumulated cystine to cysteine. The unreduced cystine forms abnormal disulfide cross-links with cytoskeletal proteins including actin and MYH9, leading to cytoskeletal collapse and cell death through a mechanism distinct from apoptosis and conventional ferroptosis. In bone diseases, inflammatory factors (IL-1β, TNF-α) and immune cell infiltration upregulate SLC7A11 in osteoclast precursors and mesenchymal stem cells, promoting pathological bone resorption and tissue degeneration. Therapeutic strategies including SLC7A11 knockdown, thiol compound supplementation, and 2-DG treatment effectively block disulfidptosis, while targeting metabolic regulators like RPN1 and TXNRD1 improves bone microstructure, offering novel interventional approaches for managing degenerative orthopedic conditions.

title: STAT1 promotes ferroptosis and inflammation in mouse hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury
summary: This study demonstrates that STAT1 is a critical transcription factor promoting ferroptosis and inflammatory damage during hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury (HIRI) through a miR-497-5p/HDAC7 regulatory axis. STAT1 protein is significantly upregulated in liver tissues during HIRI and promotes ferroptosis by suppressing miR-497-5p transcription, which leads to HDAC7 activation; HDAC7 then directly promotes both ferroptotic cell death and inflammatory responses. Genetic deletion of Stat1 markedly reduces lipid peroxidation, suppresses proinflammatory cytokine expression (IL-1β and IL-6), and improves hepatocyte viability and liver function, while knockdown of Stat1 enhances cell survival and mitigates apoptosis. The study implicates multiple signaling pathways—including JAK-STAT, NF-κB, TNF, and IL-17 pathways—in HIRI pathogenesis. These findings establish STAT1 as a potential therapeutic target for mitigating oxidative stress-induced ferroptosis and inflammation in ischemia-reperfusion injury, suggesting that inhibiting STAT1 activity could protect hepatocytes from the dual threats of ferroptotic cell death and excessive inflammatory damage.

title: Ferroptosis in the Ovarian Follicular Microenvironment: A Redox-Dependent Cell Death Pathway with Emerging Roles in PCOS, Oocyte Quality, and IVF Outcomes
summary: Ferroptosis is a redox-dependent form of regulated cell death characterized by iron-catalyzed lipid peroxidation, inactivation of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), and mitochondrial shrinkage that plays an emerging role in reproductive pathology. Dysregulation of ferroptosis in the ovarian follicular microenvironment impairs granulosa cell survival, oocyte maturation, and embryonic competence, thereby contributing to infertility in conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome. The mechanism involves multiple regulatory pathways: GPX4 and the FSP1-coenzyme Q10 axis suppress ferroptosis by preventing lipid peroxide accumulation, while ferroptosis inducers like erastin deplete antioxidant defenses by inhibiting the cystine/glutamate antiporter system Xc− and reducing glutathione production. The ovarian follicle is particularly susceptible to ferroptosis due to high polyunsaturated fatty acid content in granulosa cells, elevated reactive oxygen species and redox-active iron in follicular fluid, and oocyte dependence on precise redox signaling for developmental competence. Pharmacological interventions including ferrostatin-1, melatonin, vitamin E, and coenzyme Q10 effectively inhibit ferroptosis and may represent therapeutic strategies to improve oocyte quality and fertility outcomes.

title: Asaraldehyde suppresses non-small cell lung cancer progression via ferroptosis induction and inhibition of PI3K-AKT signaling
summary:
# Summary

Asaraldehyde suppresses non-small cell lung cancer progression through dual mechanisms of ferroptosis induction and PI3K-AKT pathway inhibition. The compound significantly reduces NSCLC cell proliferation, migration, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition by triggering ferroptosis, evidenced by increased lipid peroxidation, reactive oxygen species accumulation, and glutathione depletion. Mechanistically, asaraldehyde promotes ubiquitin-mediated degradation of AKT, thereby inhibiting the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway that is typically dysregulated in NSCLC progression. In vivo studies confirmed asaraldehyde's therapeutic efficacy, demonstrating significant tumor growth reduction alongside decreased Ki67 and AKT expression in a xenograft mouse model. The ferroptosis inhibitor ferrostatin-1 partially reversed asaraldehyde's anticancer effects, confirming ferroptosis as a critical mechanism of action. These findings suggest asaraldehyde represents a promising therapeutic strategy targeting both ferroptosis resistance and aberrant PI3K-AKT signaling in NSCLC.

title: Heme and iron toxicity in the aged spleen impairs T cell immunity through iron deprivation
summary:
# Summary

Aging of the spleen creates a toxic microenvironment rich in heme and iron that impairs T cell immunity through iron deprivation and oxidative stress. Age-related dysfunction of splenic red pulp macrophages leads to accumulation of senescent red blood cells, heme, and iron deposits, which promote oxidative stress and ferroptosis in T cells. To survive this hostile niche, T cells upregulate heme catabolism enzymes (HMOX1, BLVRB) and iron storage proteins (ferritin), while simultaneously downregulating the iron regulatory protein IRP2 to maintain a low labile iron pool. Exposure of young T cells to the aged splenic microenvironment induces aging phenotypes including reduced proliferation, elevated senescence markers (KLRG1, CD95, CD39), and increased production of inflammatory cytokines. Iron supplementation paradoxically enhances antigen-specific T cell responses in aged mice, suggesting that the iron deprivation strategy, while protective against ferroptosis, ultimately compromises T cell function. This study reveals how age-related iron and heme toxicity in splenic tissues drives systemic T cell aging and immunosenescence through iron sequestration mechanisms.

title: Influenza a(H1N1) virus infection impairs syncytiotrophoblast functions by promoting ferroptosis through the TNF-α/ISG15/EGR1 axis
summary:
# Summary

Influenza A(H1N1) virus infection impairs syncytiotrophoblast function and causes adverse pregnancy outcomes—including spontaneous abortion, preterm birth, and neonatal death—through a TNF-α/ISG15/EGR1 axis that promotes ferroptosis. H1N1 infection upregulates TNF-α expression in trophoblast cells, which triggers a positive feedback loop involving ISG15 and the transcription factor EGR1 that amplifies ferroptosis-related gene transcription. This ferroptotic process involves hallmark iron-dependent mechanisms including glutathione depletion, downregulation of the ferroptosis-suppressor genes GPX4 and SLC7A11, accumulation of lipid peroxides and intracellular iron, and mitochondrial shrinkage that ultimately inhibits trophoblast cell fusion and syncytialization. Importantly, TNF-α inhibition or pharmacologic ferroptosis suppression with ferrostatin-1 reverses these effects, restoring trophoblast fusion capacity and reducing markers of ferroptosis. These findings establish ferroptosis as a critical mechanism linking maternal H1N1 infection to placental dysfunction and adverse pregnancy outcomes, with potential therapeutic targets in blocking the TNF-α signaling pathway.

title: Maternal sleep deprivation during pregnancy induced offspring germ cells loss through ferroptosis
summary:
# Summary

Maternal sleep deprivation during pregnancy induces ferroptosis in offspring ovaries, leading to substantial germ cell loss that compromises reproductive health. Sleep-deprived pregnant mice exhibited reduced circulating estradiol, progesterone, and luteinizing hormone while showing elevated follicle-stimulating hormone, ultimately creating an unfavorable hormonal environment for fetal germ cell development. The mechanism involves disruption of meiotic progression, accumulation of ferrous iron (Fe²⁺) and reactive oxygen species, and dysregulation of ferroptosis-regulating proteins including GPX4, NRF2, ACSL4, and SLC7A11, resulting in increased lipid peroxidation and cell death. Sleep deprivation also reduced expression of critical germ cell markers and primordial follicle formation factors (MVH, LHX8, SOHLH1), ultimately decreasing the primordial follicle reserve that determines reproductive lifespan. Importantly, ferroptosis inhibition with Ferrostatin-1 effectively restored germ cell numbers, normalized ferroptosis-regulating protein expression, and preserved oocyte developmental competence, establishing ferroptosis as a therapeutic target for mitigating maternal sleep deprivation's adverse reproductive effects on offspring.

title: The “gut microbiota-ferroptosis axis”: a hypothesis for osteoarthritis pathogenesis and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

This article proposes the "gut microbiota-ferroptosis axis" as a mechanistic explanation for osteoarthritis pathogenesis, wherein dysbiotic changes in gut bacterial composition promote ferroptosis—an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death—that directly contributes to chondrocyte degeneration and cartilage extracellular matrix degradation. The mechanism involves dysbiosis-induced compromised intestinal barrier function, allowing lipopolysaccharide translocation that activates toll-like receptor 4 signaling and promotes ferroptosis, while simultaneously reducing protective metabolites like short-chain fatty acids and serotonin that normally inhibit ferroptosis. Key ferroptosis regulators identified include the cystine transporter system Xc−, glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), and iron metabolism proteins; dysbiosis-driven dysregulation of these pathways, along with mechanical stress activation of PIEZO1 channels, sensitizes chondrocytes to ferroptosis-induced cell death and matrix metalloproteinase/ADAMTS overexpression. Therapeutic interventions targeting this axis, including resveratrol, quercetin, ACSL4 inhibitors (paeonol, rosiglitazone, pioglitazone), and PIEZO1 inhibitors, show promise in suppressing ferroptosis and preserving chondrocyte viability and extracellular matrix integrity, offering novel strategies to reverse both dysbiosis and cartilage degeneration in osteoarthritis.

title: Nrf2 signaling in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: regulation of ferroptosis and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

Nrf2, a master transcriptional regulator encoded by the Nfe2L2 gene, controls cellular redox homeostasis and inflammation through activation of antioxidant response elements and induction of protective genes including HO-1, NQO1, GCLC, and SLC7A11. Under basal conditions, Nrf2 activity is tightly controlled through degradation mechanisms involving Keap1, βTrCP, and HRD1 ubiquitin ligases; however, oxidative stress and ROS can trigger Keap1 oxidation, leading to Nrf2 release and nuclear translocation. Nrf2 dysfunction compromises cellular antioxidant defenses and critically links to ferroptosis—a form of regulated cell death characterized by iron accumulation, glutathione depletion, GPX4 inactivation, and lipid peroxidation—which drives airway epithelial damage, emphysema, and inflammation in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Nrf2 directly regulates ferroptosis susceptibility by controlling iron homeostasis, the System Xc−/GSH/GPX4 pathway, and lipid peroxidation; notably, cigarette smoke generates overwhelming oxidative stress, disrupts Nrf2 expression, and dysregulates upstream signaling networks. Therapeutic approaches including Wnt3a/β-catenin activation, DJ-1 stabilization, and AMPK enhancement successfully enhance Nrf2 signaling and attenuate disease pathology, as exemplified by lithium chloride treatment which increases Nrf2 activation and HO-1/NQO1 expression to protect against emphysema.

title: The implication of aberrant NRF2 activation in management of female cancers
summary:
# Summary

NRF2 functions as a critical transcription factor regulating antioxidant and detoxifying gene expression, including GCLC, NQO1, HMOX1, and xCT, which collectively enhance cellular antioxidant capacity and glutathione biosynthesis. While transient NRF2 activation in normal cells protects against oxidative stress and ROS-induced DNA damage, persistent NRF2 hyperactivation through KEAP1 mutations or epigenetic dysregulation paradoxically promotes tumor progression in established female malignancies by enhancing cell survival, proliferation, metabolic reprogramming, and chemoresistance. NRF2-GCL axis deregulation has been specifically identified in endometriotic lesions where it contributes to pathological growth and fibrosis, suggesting shared mechanisms between endometriosis and female cancers. Multiple inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β activate transcription factors that can sustain aberrant NRF2 signaling, while targeted inhibition of NRF2 targets such as NQO1 via natural compounds like daphnetin can overcome chemoresistance by inducing ferroptosis. These findings highlight the context-dependent role of NRF2 and suggest that blocking persistent NRF2 activation represents a therapeutic strategy to sensitize resistant tumors to treatment.

title: Inhibition of Ferroptotic Toxicity by 4-Hydroxyindole
summary:
# Summary: Inhibition of Ferroptotic Toxicity by 4-Hydroxyindole

4-Hydroxyindole (4-HI) functions as a potent ferroptosis inhibitor through its radical scavenging activity, effectively protecting multiple neuronal cell lines (HT-22, N27, and RBE4) against ferroptosis induced by erastin, RSL3, and FINO2. The compound exhibits antioxidant effects via its indole ring and hydroxyl group, which work together to neutralize reactive oxygen species and prevent phospholipid peroxidation, the hallmark mechanism of ferroptotic cell death. Notably, 4-HI has been identified as a biomarker for endometriosis, with lower levels detected in endometriosis patients compared to healthy individuals, and it has been shown to inhibit both the onset and progression of the disease. In comparative studies, 4-HI demonstrated superior antiferroptotic potency compared to the standard ferroptosis inhibitor Liproxstatin-1 in some assays, though its structural analog 4-hydroxyindole-3-carbaldehyde lost protective activity due to the electron-withdrawing carbaldehyde group. These findings establish 4-HI as a promising therapeutic candidate for neuroprotective applications in neurodegenerative diseases and potentially for endometriosis treatment, with the compound naturally found in tomatoes and produced by gut bacteria.

title: Transcriptomic exploration of key genes related to mitochondria and ferroptosis in inflammatory bowel disease and experimental validation
summary:
# Summary

This study identified key ferroptosis-related genes in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) through transcriptomic analysis and experimental validation. Four critical genes were characterized: ACSL4 and IL1B were significantly upregulated in IBD and associated with adaptive immune activation and cytokine production, while AQP8 and ACSF2 were downregulated and linked to lymphocyte immunity and fatty acid metabolism respectively. ACSL4 promotes ferroptosis susceptibility by activating arachidonic acid metabolism and aberrantly enhancing the TCA cycle, which impairs AMPK-dependent mitophagy and ultimately impedes mucosal healing. The study demonstrated that mitochondrial dysfunction and ferroptosis are interconnected processes driving IBD pathogenesis, with elevated reactive oxygen species and lipid peroxidation as key pathogenic mechanisms. Notably, vitamin D and the mitochondrial antioxidant SkQ1 can inhibit ferroptosis by suppressing ACSL4 or reducing reactive oxygen species, suggesting therapeutic targets for IBD treatment.

title: Ferroptosis, autophagy, and mitochondrial dynamics: Front burners in cancer therapeutics
summary:
# Summary

This review examines ferroptosis, autophagy, and mitochondrial dynamics as interconnected mechanisms in cancer therapeutics. Ferroptosis is an iron-driven regulated cell death involving glutathione (GSH) depletion, GPX4 inactivation, lipid peroxidation, and reactive oxygen species accumulation through inhibition of the cystine-glutamate antiporter system Xc-. Mitochondrial dynamics—regulated by fusion proteins (MFN1/2, OPA1) and fission protein (DRP1)—play a critical role in ferroptosis, with iron overload disrupting the fission-fusion balance and promoting mitophagy-mediated ferroptosis. Selective autophagy pathways have opposing roles: mitophagy and ferritin autophagy promote ferroptosis by eliminating protective mitochondria and iron storage, while non-selective autophagy and lipophagy inhibit ferroptosis by removing damaged organelles and reducing oxidative damage. Multiple ferroptosis inducers (erastin, sulfasalazine, sorafenib) and GSH synthesis inhibitors (buthionine sulfoximine) enhance chemotherapy efficacy in various cancers, with erastin derivatives showing improved pharmacological properties. Heat shock proteins confer ferroptosis resistance, suggesting that modulating autophagy and mitochondrial function could sensitize tumors to ferroptosis-based therapeutic strategies.

title: Phloretin inhibits ferroptosis by restoring the antioxidant capacity of bovine adipose and muscle cells via the AMPK-PPAR signaling pathway
summary:
# Summary

Phloretin, a polyphenolic compound found in apples, potently inhibits ferroptosis in bovine adipose and muscle cells by restoring antioxidant capacity through activation of the AMPK-PPAR signaling pathway. The compound reduces hallmark ferroptosis markers including intracellular iron (Fe2+), malondialdehyde (MDA), and reactive oxygen species while simultaneously upregulating ferroptosis-suppressing genes such as Gpx4 and Fth1 and antioxidant enzymes including GSH-Px, catalase, and superoxide dismutase. High-fat diet-induced oxidative stress triggers ferroptosis in adipose and muscle tissues by promoting expression of ferroptosis-promoting factors like ACSL4 and PTGS2; phloretin reverses these pathological changes and suppresses obesity-related metabolic dysfunction. The protective effects operate via AMPK pathway activation, which downstream activates PPAR signaling to coordinately suppress lipid peroxidation and restore mitochondrial membrane potential and cellular redox homeostasis. These findings establish phloretin as a promising anti-ferroptotic agent with implications for treating metabolic disorders and tissues susceptible to ferroptosis-mediated pathology.

title: Trained Immunity Exacerbates Inflammatory Arthritis Progression via Promoting Synovial Fibroblast Ferroptotic Resistance
summary:
# Summary

β-glucan induces trained immunity in macrophages, which exacerbates inflammatory arthritis by promoting a fibro-inflammatory phenotype in synovial fibroblasts through elevated IL-1β production and enhanced glycolysis. Trained macrophages protect arthritis-derived fibroblasts from ferroptosis via IL-1β signaling, which upregulates NAT10-mediated ac4C modification of FSP1 mRNA, thereby increasing ferroptosis suppressor protein 1 expression and resistance to cell death. Blocking ferroptosis suppressors alone has limited therapeutic benefit, but combining FSP1 inhibition with NAT10 inhibition (Remodelin) effectively alleviates synovial inflammation, reduces cartilage and bone damage, and shifts cytokine production toward an anti-inflammatory profile. This work demonstrates that trained immunity-driven ferroptotic resistance of fibroblasts represents a critical mechanism perpetuating inflammatory arthritis progression, suggesting dual targeting of ferroptosis resistance and macrophage reprogramming as a promising therapeutic strategy.

title: Lactylation-mediated ferroptosis: A novel mechanism and therapeutic prospects in human diseases (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Lysine lactylation (Kla), an emerging post-translational modification driven by lactate accumulation, regulates ferroptosis through multiple epigenetic and metabolic mechanisms with significant implications for endometriosis and other diseases. Lactate accumulates during metabolic stress and directly drives histone lactylation modifications (H3K18la, H3K14la, H4K8la, H4K12la) that control ferroptosis by altering expression of key regulatory genes. In endometriosis specifically, H3K18la upregulates the methyltransferase METTL3, which promotes HIF1A and HMOX1 expression, while H3K18la also activates protective pathways through ZFP64-mediated upregulation of GCH1 and FTH1, and through NFS1 activation to maintain iron homeostasis—all suppressing ferroptosis. Additionally, lactate-driven lactylation directly impairs ferroptosis suppressors like GPX4 (via K218/K228 modification) while promoting glycolytic feedback through H4K8la-mediated PFKFB3 stabilization. These lactylation-mediated mechanisms simultaneously drive disease progression through ferroptosis resistance in inflammatory contexts and cancer, suggesting that targeting lactate-Kla signaling represents a novel therapeutic strategy for endometriosis and other pathologies characterized by dysregulated ferroptosis.

title: Targeting the ROS-ferroptosis-inflammation cycle with a nanozyme-functionalized hydrogel for intervertebral disc repair
summary: Intervertebral disc degeneration is driven by a vicious cycle involving reactive oxygen species (ROS), ferroptosis, and inflammation, where excessive ROS production leads to ferroptosis of nucleus pulposus cells, which in turn amplifies inflammatory responses through the IL6/STAT3 axis. Severe disc degeneration is characterized by elevated ferroptosis driver genes (ACSL4, SLC39A14, CHAC1) and reduced ferroptosis suppressor genes (GPX4, FTH1, HSPB1), along with increased lipid peroxidation markers and pro-inflammatory cytokines. To address this pathology, researchers developed a nanozyme-functionalized hydrogel (PG@MBC) that combines black phosphorus nanosheets with cerium oxide, which scavenges ROS through a Ce(III)/Ce(IV) redox cycle and suppresses IL6 expression via HuR-mediated mRNA stabilization. The hydrogel is ROS-responsive, with excess ROS cleaving boronic ester bonds to trigger targeted release of the nanozyme to nucleus pulposus cells through homologous membrane interactions, thereby breaking the ROS-ferroptosis-inflammation feedback loop. By simultaneously inhibiting ferroptosis and inflammation while scavenging ROS, the PG@MBC hydrogel creates a suitable microenvironment that promotes extracellular matrix restoration and nucleus pulposus regeneration, effectively treating intervertebral disc degeneration.

title: The mechanism study of targeting DPP4 in regulating ferroptosis and its influence on endometrial receptivity in PCOS
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies dipeptidyl peptidase 4 (DPP4) as a key regulator of ferroptosis in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)-associated endometrial dysfunction. In PCOS, elevated oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction trigger ferroptosis—an iron-dependent cell death pathway characterized by iron accumulation, glutathione depletion, and GPX4 downregulation—which impairs endometrial receptivity and decidualization capacity. DPP4 drives this ferroptotic process through hormone receptor upregulation, and inhibiting DPP4 with sitagliptin suppresses ferroptosis markers while restoring decidualization responses and endometrial receptivity in both animal models and human PCOS specimens. Clinical evaluation confirmed that sitagliptin treatment significantly reversed ferroptosis-related endometrial pathology in PCOS patients and improved reproductive outcomes, including higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates. These findings establish ferroptosis as a novel therapeutic target in PCOS-related infertility and demonstrate that DPP4 inhibition ameliorates PCOS-associated endometrial dysfunction through ferroptosis suppression, offering a promising mechanistic basis for improving fertility in PCOS patients.

title: Targeting Lactylation Offers Therapy to Reverse Cell Death Resistance
summary: Lactylation, a lactate-derived post-translational modification catalyzed by acetyltransferases such as p300 and KAT7, emerges as a critical metabolic regulator that suppresses multiple forms of regulated cell death including ferroptosis, cuproptosis, and apoptosis. Through modification of histone and non-histone proteins, lactylation confers resistance to ferroptosis by upregulating glutathione synthesis genes (GCLM/GCLC), activating the antioxidant transcription factor NRF2 via PRDX1 modification, and depleting labile iron through NFS1-driven iron-sulfur cluster biogenesis. In cuproptosis, lactylation of IGF-1R stabilizes the tumor suppressor CDKN2A, while lactylation of NUDT21 destabilizes the copper toxicity executor FDX1. Additionally, caspase-3 lactylation directly inhibits apoptotic proteolytic activation. The article identifies lactate accumulation in inflammatory microenvironments as a key driver of this protective lactylation signature, suggesting that targeting lactate metabolism or lactylation-catalyzing enzymes could reverse cell death resistance and restore therapeutic sensitivity in diseases characterized by these metabolic adaptations.

title: Iron metabolism disorder promotes postovulatory oocyte aging by inducing oxidative stress damage
summary:
# Summary

Iron metabolism disorder is a critical driver of postovulatory oocyte aging through ferroptosis-mediated oxidative stress. Following ovulation, oviductal heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) is elevated and catalyzes iron release from heme degradation, while oocytes develop iron homeostasis dysfunction characterized by increased iron importers (DMT1 and transferrin receptor 1), decreased iron exporters (ferroportin1), and reduced iron storage protein ferritin heavy chain. This dysregulation causes excessive cytosolic and mitochondrial iron accumulation that fuels Fenton reactions, generating hydroxyl radicals that trigger lipid peroxidation and DNA damage while depleting the anti-aging factor Sirt1. Iron chelation therapy with deferoxamine or HO-1 inhibition with zinc protoporphyrin effectively alleviates these aging changes and restores oocyte competence, demonstrating that iron-dependent ferroptosis is mechanistically central to postovulatory oocyte deterioration. The process involves impaired ferritinophagy regulation through decreased O-GlcNAcylation and reduced ferritin heavy chain stability, allowing uncontrolled labile iron pool expansion. These findings establish iron metabolism as a therapeutic target for preventing age-related oocyte dysfunction and improving reproductive outcomes.

title: Ferroptosis and cancer: when iron turns against tumors
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary:
# Summary

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by the accumulation of lipid peroxides, distinct from apoptosis and potentially exploitable as a cancer treatment strategy. Ferrous iron (Fe(II)) catalyzes the Fenton reaction to generate reactive oxygen species that trigger lipid peroxidation through enzymes like ACSL4, LPCAT3, and ALOX, while the loss of GPX4 function—a key lipid hydroperoxide reducer—permits unchecked ferroptotic progression. Ferroptosis is governed by a balance between pro-ferroptotic drivers (NCOA4-mediated ferritinophagy, lipid peroxidation enzymes) and ferroptosis suppressors (GPX4, FSP1, DHODH, and NRF2), with cancer cells actively upregulating antioxidant defenses through NRF2 to promote iron addiction and survival. Chronic iron overload states and exposure to iron-rich environmental agents like asbestos fibers increase cancer risk by promoting oxidative DNA damage and ferroptosis in macrophages, which then release ferritin-containing exosomes that fuel malignant transformation. Ferroptosis inducers such as erastin inhibit the xCT cystine/glutamate antiporter, depleting glutathione and inactivating GPX4, offering a promising approach to selectively kill RAS-mutated and other cancer cells resistant to conventional therapies.

title: Ferroptosis in human reproductive tract infections and associated disorders: mechanisms and emerging therapeutic opportunities
summary:
# Summary

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death driven by lipid peroxidation, emerges as a critical pathological mechanism in reproductive tract infections and associated disorders. The process is mechanistically centered on the disruption of the System Xc−–GSH–GPX4 antioxidant axis and dysregulation of iron metabolism, wherein intracellular iron overload catalyzes Fenton reactions that generate excessive reactive oxygen species, ultimately promoting lipid peroxidation and cellular damage. In HIV infection specifically, ferroptosis contributes significantly to CD4+ T cell depletion through multiple viral mechanisms: the HIV-1 Tat protein promotes ferroptosis via the miR-204-ACSL4 signaling axis and interferes with selenium metabolism to reduce GPX4 expression, while the gp120 capsid protein disrupts iron homeostasis. Ferroptosis exacerbates various reproductive pathologies including Staphylococcus aureus-induced endometritis, mastitis, and complications from SARS-CoV-2 infection, while also playing roles in cervical carcinogenesis and male infertility. The ferroptotic cascade activates the NLRP3 inflammasome, fostering a chronic inflammatory microenvironment that suppresses immune reconstitution and CD4+ T cell function. These findings highlight ferroptosis as a therapeutic target with potential for intervention through ferroptosis inhibitors to protect host cells or, conversely, ferroptosis inducers to selectively eliminate pathogens.

title: Oviductus Ranae alleviates D-galactose-induced ovarian aging by inhibiting ferroptosis and regulating the GPX4/ACSL4 pathway
summary: This study demonstrates that Oviductus Ranae (OR), a traditional Chinese medicine, protects ovarian function and prevents aging by inhibiting ferroptosis through the GPX4/ACSL4 signaling axis. Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death driven by lipid peroxidation, plays a central role in D-galactose-induced ovarian aging in granulosa cells. OR treatment downregulates pro-ferroptotic factors (ACSL4, LPCAT3, ALOX12) while upregulating anti-ferroptotic regulators (GPX4, SLC7A11, GSS, FTH1, FTL), thereby reducing iron deposition and oxidative stress while elevating glutathione levels. Additionally, OR enhances ovarian follicular development, improves serum hormone levels, reduces reactive oxygen species production, and suppresses apoptotic pathways (Bax, caspase-3/9, p53) while promoting the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2 and activating protective MAPK signaling. These findings reveal that OR exerts multimodal protective effects on ovarian granulosa cells by stabilizing mitochondrial function, improving antioxidant defenses, and preventing ferroptosis-mediated ovarian aging, with implications for treating age-related reproductive disorders including endometriosis and PCOS.

title: Targeting Ferroptosis to Overcome Drug Resistance in Cancer: Molecular Mechanisms and Therapeutic Prospects
summary:
# Summary

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, representing a potential therapeutic avenue for overcoming drug resistance in cancer. Iron homeostasis, controlled by transferrin, ferritin, and ferroportin, plays a central role in ferroptosis, with hepcidin regulation of ferroportin directly influencing cellular iron accumulation. Multiple molecular pathways suppress ferroptosis through antioxidant defenses: the system Xc- transporter imports cystine for glutathione (GSH) synthesis, while glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) detoxifies lipid hydroperoxides; conversely, inhibitors like erastin and p53 block these protective pathways to induce ferroptosis. Ferroptosis execution involves lipid peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids, promoted by ACSL4 and LPCAT3, and catalyzed by lipoxygenases, while the MAPK signaling pathway modulates ferroptosis through effects on both ERK phosphorylation and GPX4 expression. Multiple ferroptosis-inducing compounds—including β-elemene, simvastatin, orlistat, dictamnine, and natural products like heteronemin and micheliolide—achieve therapeutic effects by suppressing MAPK signaling or directly downregulating antioxidant defenses, offering promising strategies for cancer treatment.

title: Lithium exposure causes ferroptosis to induce unexplained miscarriage through non-canonical MBOAT1 pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that lithium exposure induces unexplained miscarriage through ferroptosis in placental trophoblast cells via a non-canonical MBOAT1 pathway. Lithium downregulates estrogen receptor (ER) signaling, which suppresses transcription of METTL1 and MBOAT1; this reduction in METTL1-mediated m7G modification destabilizes MBOAT1 mRNA, ultimately decreasing MBOAT1 protein levels and its protective role in limiting polyunsaturated fatty acid accumulation. The resulting increase in intracellular iron and lipid peroxidation, combined with reduced antioxidant defenses (SOD and GPx activity), triggers GPX4-independent ferroptosis in placental tissue. In both human patients with unexplained miscarriage and experimental mouse models, elevated serum lithium levels correlate with higher placental ferroptosis markers (elevated Fe2+ and MDA, reduced GPx activity) and miscarriage rates, while MBOAT1 supplementation effectively suppresses ferroptosis and prevents pregnancy loss. These findings establish ferroptosis as a key mechanism linking lithium exposure to adverse reproductive outcomes and implicate the MBOAT1 pathway as a potential therapeutic target.

title: Ferroptosis in ischemia-reperfusion injury: molecular mechanisms and therapeutic strategies
summary: Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of programmed cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation, emerges as a critical mechanism in ischemia-reperfusion injury across multiple organs including the heart, brain, liver, and kidney. During reperfusion, reactive oxygen species surge and trigger ferroptotic pathways that intersect with oxidative stress, immune-inflammatory responses, and autophagy, collectively contributing to tissue damage. The molecular regulation of ferroptosis involves key players including system Xc⁻, GPX4, ACSL4, TfR1, and NCOA4, which regulate iron homeostasis and lipid peroxidation. The pathological basis of ferroptosis is rooted in disturbances of energy metabolism, iron homeostasis, and mitochondrial integrity, making it a multifaceted process amenable to therapeutic targeting. Interventions that modulate these ferroptotic regulators represent promising therapeutic strategies to improve tissue recovery and reduce ischemia-reperfusion injury.

title: NEDD4 suppresses ferroptosis in lung ischemia-reperfusion injury by ubiquitinating and degrading SLC1A5
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies the E3 ubiquitin ligase NEDD4 as a critical suppressor of ferroptosis in lung ischemia-reperfusion injury (LIRI). NEDD4 directly interacts with the glutamine transporter SLC1A5 at the plasma membrane and promotes its K48-linked ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, thereby reducing ferroptotic cell death. NEDD4 expression is downregulated in both murine LIRI models and hypoxia-reoxygenation (H/R)-injured pulmonary epithelial cells, and NEDD4 deficiency exacerbates lung injury, apoptosis, and ferroptosis through elevated reactive oxygen species, lipid peroxidation, and iron overload. Conversely, NEDD4 overexpression rescues H/R-induced ferroptosis by reducing oxidative stress, restoring expression of ferroptosis inhibitors GPX4 and SLC7A11, and suppressing SLC1A5-mediated glutamine uptake. Functional studies confirm that SLC1A5 overexpression reverses NEDD4's protective effects, establishing SLC1A5 as the key mediator of NEDD4's anti-ferroptotic mechanism. These findings reveal a novel therapeutic target for LIRI and extend NEDD4's established cytoprotective roles in other pathologies including endometriosis.

title: Plasma-Derived Exosomal hsa-miR-3677-3p Induces Ferroptosis in Neurons by Targeting ABCB8 in Perioperative Neurocognitive Disorders After Prostate Surgery
summary: This study identifies plasma-derived exosomal hsa-miR-3677-3p as a key mediator of ferroptosis in neurons following perioperative neurocognitive disorders. The microRNA directly targets ABCB8, a mitochondrial transporter essential for iron homeostasis, leading to iron accumulation, mitochondrial oxidative stress, and neuronal damage characterized by lipid peroxidation and impaired mitochondrial function. Elevated hsa-miR-3677-3p levels in patient plasma exosomes correlate with cognitive decline and show diagnostic potential for distinguishing affected individuals. In vitro overexpression of the microRNA recapitulates this pathological cascade, reducing ABCB8 expression, promoting intracellular iron accumulation, elevating markers of oxidative stress (malondialdehyde and mitochondrial lipid peroxidation), and depleting antioxidant capacity. Critically, treatment with the ferroptosis inhibitor Ferrostatin-1 reverses these effects, restoring mitochondrial function and reducing oxidative damage, suggesting ferroptosis inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy. These findings establish an exosome-microRNA-ferroptosis axis linking peripheral biomarkers to neuronal injury in postoperative cognitive complications.

title: N-oxide-based and trioxolane-based fluorescent probes for monitoring labile iron and labile heme: an update
journal: Journal of Clinical Biochemistry and Nutrition
summary:
# Summary

This review examines N-oxide-based and trioxolane-based fluorescent probes for detecting labile iron and heme, key mediators of ferroptosis and oxidative stress. Iron mediates the Fenton reaction and catalyzes lipid peroxidation, hallmarks of ferroptosis, while labile heme accumulation is associated with hemolysis, malaria, sickle cell disease, and porphyria. The authors developed organelle-targeted probes including RhoNox variants for Fe(II) detection and H-FluNox for selective heme monitoring, revealing that ferroptosis involves elevated labile Fe(II) and heme primarily in the endoplasmic reticulum and lysosomes. Notably, N-oxide-based probes not only detect but also inhibit ferroptosis through Fe(II) oxidation and sequestration, whereas 1,2,4-trioxolane compounds paradoxically induce ferroptosis. The probes successfully revealed new mechanistic insights, including the role of KEAP1/NRF2 signaling in iron homeostasis and the heme-exporting functions of ABCG2, positioning these tools as both diagnostic and potential therapeutic agents for iron-dependent pathological conditions.

title: Glutathione peroxidase 4 as an emerging therapeutic target in osteoarthritis: focus on ferroptosis
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary: Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death, emerges as a critical pathogenic mechanism in osteoarthritis, where altered glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) expression and activity contribute to chondrocyte death and cartilage degradation. GPX4, which normally inhibits ferroptosis by catalyzing the reduction of lipid peroxides using glutathione as a cofactor, is significantly downregulated in osteoarthritic cartilage in response to inflammatory cytokines like interleukin-1β and pathological iron accumulation. The ferroptotic pathway is initiated when iron catalyzes the Fenton reaction, generating reactive oxygen species that peroxidize polyunsaturated fatty acids in cell membranes, ultimately causing cell death through accumulation of toxic lipid hydroperoxides. Both mechanical overload and chronic inflammation activate this cascade, with mechanical stress triggering ferroptosis through the Piezo1 ion channel-calcium axis. Multiple therapeutic strategies—including ferroptosis inhibitors such as ferrostatin-1, GPX4 overexpression, and activation of the System Xc⁻-GSH-GPX4 antioxidant axis through SLC7A11 or Nrf2—successfully reduce chondrocyte death, restore cartilage morphology, and suppress the expression of matrix-degrading enzymes, suggesting GPX4 as a promising therapeutic target for osteoarthritis intervention.

title: Oxidative Stress and SIRT1-Nrf2 Anti-Ferroptotic Pathways in Granulosa Cells: A Molecular Key to Follicular Atresia and Ovarian Aging
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress and ferroptosis emerge as critical mechanisms driving granulosa cell dysfunction and follicular atresia through iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and reactive oxygen species accumulation. The SIRT1-Nrf2 signaling axis acts as the primary cellular defense, with SIRT1 promoting mitochondrial biogenesis through FOXO3 and PGC-1α deacetylation while Nrf2 transcriptionally activates antioxidant enzymes including SOD2, HO-1, NQO1, and the ferroptosis inhibitor GPX4. Disruption of this protective pathway due to declining NAD+ pools during ovarian aging accelerates granulosa cell senescence, premature follicular depletion, and the production of inflammatory mediators that further compromise ovarian function. Pharmaceutical and nutraceutical interventions including metformin, melatonin, resveratrol, and NAD+ boosters can reactivate SIRT1-Nrf2 signaling to restore mitochondrial fitness and ferroptosis resistance. Additionally, FSH-stimulated cAMP-PKA cascades regulate metabolic pathways through AMPK, AKT, and mTOR to support steroidogenesis and follicular development, while oocyte-derived GDF9 and BMP15 maintain granulosa cell identity through SMAD signaling and inhibition of premature luteinization.

title: 2D nanomaterial-triggered ferroptosis in cancer therapy
summary:
# Summary: 2D Nanomaterial-Triggered Ferroptosis in Cancer Therapy

Two-dimensional nanomaterials, particularly iron-based variants, induce ferroptosis—an iron-dependent, lipid peroxidation-driven form of cell death—by releasing Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ ions and elevating intracellular reactive oxygen species to disrupt cellular redox homeostasis. Unlike traditional apoptotic pathways to which cancer cells develop resistance, ferroptosis represents a mechanistically distinct therapeutic approach, with tumor cells exhibiting heightened susceptibility through multiple regulatory axes including the System Xc⁻–GSH–GPX4 antioxidant pathway, lipoxygenase-mediated lipid oxidation, and emerging ferroptosis suppressor systems (FSP1-CoQ10H₂, GCH1/BH4, and DHODH). Beyond direct cytotoxicity, ferroptosis triggers immunogenicity by releasing damage-associated molecular patterns that activate dendritic cells and promote CD8⁺ T cell-mediated antitumor immunity, establishing bidirectional crosstalk with the tumor microenvironment. These findings position 2D nanomaterial-induced ferroptosis as a multi-pathway cancer therapeutic strategy with both intrinsic and immunological advantages, while ferroptosis mechanisms are also implicated in pathogenic roles in autoimmune and neurodegenerative diseases, highlighting the necessity for selective modulation in therapeutic applications.

title: Vitamin E Alleviates Oxidative Damage and Attenuates Ferroptosis Caused by Prolonged Contraction of the Ciliary Muscle by Activating ACOT7
summary:
# Summary

Prolonged contraction of ciliary muscle cells, induced by carbachol, triggers ferroptosis through a cascade of cellular damage mechanisms including adenosine triphosphate depletion, reactive oxygen species accumulation, and mitochondrial dysfunction characterized by reduced cristae density and membrane potential collapse. This pathological contraction upregulates ferroptosis-promoting genes (ACSL4, PTSG2, TFR) while downregulating protective factors (GPX4, FTH1, xCT), simultaneously increasing intracellular iron and malondialdehyde while depleting glutathione and antioxidant enzyme activity. Vitamin E alleviates these effects by activating acetyl-coenzyme A thiosterase 7 (ACOT7), a critical mediator of its cytoprotective action, effectively reducing oxidative stress and ferroptosis. Ferrostatin-1, a ferroptosis inhibitor, confirmed the ferroptotic mechanism by reversing carbachol-induced transcriptional changes and restoring cell viability, glutathione content, and mitochondrial integrity. These findings establish vitamin E and ACOT7 activation as therapeutic targets for conditions involving excessive smooth muscle contraction and ferroptosis-related oxidative damage.

title: Artesunate nanoliposomes in hepatocellular carcinoma: mechanistic ferroptosis induction and translational opportunities
summary:
# Summary

Artesunate nanoliposomes suppress hepatocellular carcinoma progression through multiple converging mechanisms centered on ferroptosis induction. The antimalarial agent artesunate interacts with intracellular iron ions to generate excessive reactive oxygen species, which drives ferroptosis through lipid peroxidation and membrane disruption. Artesunate simultaneously targets iron metabolism by promoting ferritin degradation, inhibiting the transferrin receptor-HSPA9 interaction, and blocking the System Xc⁻/glutathione/GPX4 antioxidant defense pathway, thereby depleting cellular cystine and glutathione needed to suppress ferroptosis. Additionally, artesunate activates the p53 tumor suppressor pathway through DNA damage induction, which transcriptionally represses SLC7A11 and activates ALOX12 to amplify lipid peroxidation. Artesunate also inhibits the Nrf2-ATF4-SLC7A11 positive feedback loop that normally confers ferroptosis resistance. The nanoliposomal delivery system enhances drug solubility and intratumoral concentration, improving therapeutic efficacy while these interconnected mechanisms collectively overcome ferroptosis resistance and suppress tumor growth.

title: Elevated maternal pre-transfer serum lipid peroxidation is associated with implantation failure and early pregnancy loss
summary: Elevated maternal serum lipid peroxidation, marked by increased malondialdehyde (MDA) and non-heme iron levels, is independently associated with recurrent implantation failure, implantation failure, and early pregnancy loss in assisted reproductive technology patients. Iron accumulation and reactive oxygen species trigger lipid peroxidation through both enzymatic pathways (ACSL4, ALOXs) and the Fenton reaction, ultimately inducing ferroptosis and impairing endometrial receptivity and decidualization. Oxidative lipid metabolites including hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids (12-HETE, 15-HETE) amplify oxidative damage by regulating inflammatory factors, while deficiency of the protective enzyme GPX4 in endometrial epithelial cells disrupts lipid peroxidation balance. Patients experiencing pregnancy failure demonstrated significantly elevated serum MDA, non-heme iron, and HETE levels alongside reduced superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity compared to successful pregnancies, findings corroborated by a murine erastin model showing impaired endometrial receptivity. These results identify dysregulated lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis as critical mechanisms underlying pregnancy complications and suggest ferroptosis regulation as a potential therapeutic target for improving implantation outcomes.

title: Natural products modulate programmed cell death signaling mechanism for treating endometriosis: a review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by dysregulation of programmed cell death (PCD), including impaired apoptosis and autophagy, which contributes to the disease's hallmark symptoms of dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and reduced fertility. Ectopic endometrial cells evade apoptosis through multiple mechanisms: the mitochondrial pathway exhibits Bcl-2/Bax imbalance that inhibits caspase-9 activation, while the death receptor pathway involves elevated FasL and TNF-α that paradoxically induce immune cell apoptosis, allowing ectopic cells to escape immune surveillance. Pro-survival signaling cascades including PI3K/Akt, MAPK, and NF-κB pathways cooperate to suppress apoptosis and promote inflammatory cytokine production (IL-6) and anti-apoptotic protein expression. In early endometriosis, reduced autophagy driven by high estrogen, progesterone resistance, and chronic inflammation further enables ectopic cell survival, while ferroptosis—an iron-dependent cell death mechanism—plays a paradoxical role, being suppressed in early disease but potentially therapeutic in advanced stages. Natural products such as apigenin and baicalein restore PCD by simultaneously inhibiting PI3K/Akt and MAPK pathways while reducing Bcl-2 expression, offering promising therapeutic approaches to rebalance cell death signaling and eliminate ectopic lesions.

title: Cuproptosis: Biomarkers, Mechanisms and Treatments in Diseases
summary:
# Summary: Cuproptosis: Biomarkers, Mechanisms and Treatments

Copper is an essential trace element distributed throughout the body, particularly in the liver, brain, and heart, where it serves as a critical cofactor for metalloenzymes including cytochrome c oxidase, superoxide dismutase, and ceruloplasmin that regulate cellular energy metabolism, antioxidant defense, and neurotransmitter synthesis. Cuproptosis is a novel form of programmed cell death triggered by excessive copper accumulation, wherein reduced copper ions directly target and oligomerize tricarboxylic acid cycle enzymes through a ferridoxin (FDX1)-dependent mechanism, distinct from apoptosis and reversible by copper chelating agents. Copper dysregulation and aberrant cuproptosis have been implicated in diverse pathological conditions including tumor onset and progression, neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease, cardiovascular diseases, and metabolic disorders. Copper homeostasis is tightly controlled through a sophisticated network of transporters (CTR1, DMT1, ATP7A/B), chaperones (ATOX1, CCS, COX17), and regulatory mechanisms including transcription factor-mediated responses by Sp1, MTF-1, and the antioxidant sensor Nrf2, which coordinate uptake, sequestration, and excretion. The multifaceted physiological roles of copper—extending beyond enzymatic functions to include regulation of the MEK/ERK and Notch signaling pathways, angiogenesis, and immune cell activation—underline both its essential nature and the pathogenic potential of dysregulation. Understanding cuproptosis mechanisms and biomarkers provides new therapeutic opportunities for diseases characterized by oxidative stress and copper imbalance

title: Leonurine alleviates lung ischemia–reperfusion injury through suppression of ferroptosis via RORα in male mice
summary: Leonurine, an alkaloid from Leonurus japonicus, alleviates lung ischemia-reperfusion injury in male mice by suppressing ferroptosis through activation of the RORα/Nrf2/GPX4 signaling axis. The compound directly binds RORα, a nuclear transcription factor involved in lipid metabolism and immune regulation, which subsequently activates Nrf2-mediated antioxidant defense pathways and upregulates GPX4, a critical ferroptosis-inhibitor enzyme. Through these mechanisms, leonurine reduces ferroptosis hallmarks including lipid peroxidation, iron accumulation, and oxidative stress markers (4-HNE and PTGS2), while improving lung histopathology, decreasing pulmonary edema, reducing neutrophil infiltration, and restoring vascular integrity. Genetic evidence demonstrates that RORα is essential for leonurine's protective effects, as RORα-knockout mice subjected to ischemia-reperfusion injury exhibited severe lung damage unresponsive to leonurine treatment. These findings establish ferroptosis as a pathogenic driver of ischemia-reperfusion injury and identify the RORα/Nrf2/GPX4 pathway as a therapeutic target for this condition, with potential implications for understanding ferroptosis mechanisms in oxidative stress-related diseases.

title: Ferroptosis in gouty arthritis: a potential therapeutic strategy
summary:
# Summary

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of programmed cell death, has emerged as a potential therapeutic target in gouty arthritis, a chronic inflammatory disease triggered by monosodium urate crystal deposition resulting from abnormalities in purine metabolism and impaired renal uric acid excretion. Iron homeostasis directly influences gout pathogenesis, as elevated systemic iron levels correlate with increased disease flare frequency and promote synovial inflammation, whereas iron chelation therapy reduces inflammatory responses. The mechanistic link involves iron-driven lipid peroxidation through Fenton chemistry, which generates reactive oxygen species that damage cellular membranes and trigger ferroptotic cell death; this process is counterbalanced by ferroptosis suppressors including glutathione peroxidase 4, the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway, and ferroptosis suppressor protein 1. Monosodium urate crystals activate the innate immune system via pattern recognition receptors and the NLRP3 inflammasome, driving nuclear factor-κB-mediated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β and tumor necrosis factor-α. By modulating ferroptosis through iron regulation or ferroptosis suppressor manipulation, novel therapeutic strategies could complement current treatments such as colchicine and glucocorticoids to more effectively control gouty arthritis inflammation and joint destruction.

title: Effects of curcumol on ferroptosis and tube forming ability of hepatic sinus endothelial cells
summary: Curcumol induces ferroptosis in hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells through activation of the P53-TFR1-FTH1 signaling axis, which increases iron uptake while simultaneously decreasing iron storage capacity, leading to pathological iron accumulation. This iron overload generates reactive oxygen species via the Fenton reaction, which is further amplified by curcumol's suppression of the antioxidant machinery, including downregulation of the cystine transporter SLC7A11 and the peroxidase GPX4. The resulting ferroptosis effectively inhibits endothelial cell proliferation, migration, and angiogenic tube formation, thereby disrupting the pathological neovascularization that accompanies hepatic fibrosis. Importantly, P53 is the critical mediator of these effects, as P53 knockout rescues both iron deposition and ferroptosis induction, while ferroptosis inhibitors can similarly block curcumol's anti-angiogenic effects. These findings suggest that ferroptosis represents a novel therapeutic mechanism by which curcumol may exert antifibrotic effects through targeting endothelial cell dysfunction, a key early event in hepatic fibrosis development.

title: Dihydroartemisinin Alleviates Neuronal Damage and Seizures in Epileptic Mice by Inhibiting Ferroptosis via the SIRT1/FOXO1/SLC7A11/GPX4 Pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dihydroartemisinin (DHA) alleviates seizures and hippocampal neuronal damage in epileptic mice by inhibiting ferroptosis through activation of the SIRT1/FOXO1/SLC7A11/GPX4 signaling pathway. In kainic acid-induced epileptic mice, DHA activates SIRT1, which reduces acetylation of FOXO1 and increases its transcriptional activity to upregulate SLC7A11 and GPX4 expression, thereby suppressing ferroptosis in hippocampal neurons. Treatment with DHA significantly reduced spontaneous recurrent seizures, prolonged seizure latency, decreased hippocampal neuronal loss in CA1, CA3, and dentate gyrus regions, and improved memory and learning deficits. At the molecular level, DHA reversed the pathological signature of epilepsy-induced ferroptosis by reducing elevated iron levels and reactive oxygen species while restoring antioxidant markers (glutathione and superoxide dismutase) and suppressing lipid peroxidation markers. These findings establish ferroptosis as a key mechanism in epilepsy pathogenesis and identify DHA as a promising neuroprotective agent that prevents seizures and cognitive dysfunction through ferroptosis inhibition.

title: Ferroptosis and immunity: rewiring the tumor microenvironment for therapy
summary:
# Ferroptosis and Immunity in the Tumor Microenvironment

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent programmed cell death driven by lipid peroxidation, plays a complex dual role in cancer biology by both suppressing tumor growth and promoting immune evasion within the tumor microenvironment. The process is initiated when iron accumulation triggers reactive oxygen species production, leading to uncontrolled lipid peroxidation that is normally prevented by the antioxidant enzyme GPX4, which is inactivated when cellular glutathione becomes depleted. Critical regulatory proteins including SLC7A11, ACSL4, and LPCAT3 facilitate ferroptosis by modulating lipid metabolism, while protective mechanisms such as the FSP1/CoQ10 axis can be suppressed through Nrf2 inhibition or glucose metabolism disruption to enhance ferroptosis sensitivity. Although ferroptosis can function as a context-dependent immunogenic cell death that enhances immune checkpoint inhibitor efficacy and CD8+ T cell responses, it simultaneously promotes immunosuppressive signals including prostaglandin E2 and damage-associated molecular patterns that support tumor progression. Therapeutic strategies targeting ferroptosis pathways, particularly through modulation of ACSL4 expression via the Hippo signaling pathway and key lipid substrates like arachidonic acid, offer promising avenues to overcome cancer treatment resistance by rewiring the immunological landscape.
