title: Yishen Gushu formula exerts osteoprotective effects in OVX-Induced PMOP rats: role of ferroptosis Inhibition and iron metabolism correction
summary:
# Summary

Postmenopausal osteoporosis develops through ferroptosis-driven bone damage triggered by estrogen deficiency, which downregulates the antioxidant enzyme GPX4 and disrupts iron metabolism through increased ferritin export via FPN1. This leads to ferroptosis acceleration via the NCOA4-FTH1 axis, which promotes ferritinophagy, releases labile iron ions, and generates reactive oxygen species that overwhelm cellular antioxidant defenses. Yishen Gushu Formula (YSGSF), a traditional Chinese medicine compound, treats postmenopausal osteoporosis by inhibiting ferroptosis through downregulation of NCOA4 and upregulation of the protective proteins FTH1, GPX4, and SLC7A11, thereby reducing iron accumulation and oxidative stress in bone tissue. In both OVX-induced rat models and Erastin-treated osteoblasts, YSGSF improved bone metabolism and restored redox balance by correcting iron metabolism disorders and suppressing reactive oxygen species and malondialdehyde production. These findings establish ferroptosis inhibition and iron metabolism correction as the key therapeutic mechanisms by which YSGSF exerts osteoprotective effects in postmenopausal osteoporosis.

title: Mechanisms Driving Resistance to Oxidative Stress During Endometrial Stromal Cell Decidualization
summary: During early pregnancy, endometrial stromal cells undergo decidualization, a progesterone- and cAMP-driven process essential for implantation, placentation, and immune tolerance. To tolerate the cellular reprogramming required during this transformation, stromal cells must resist oxidative stress by activating robust antioxidant defenses—a capability that distinguishes eutherian mammals from marsupials, whose stromal cells instead activate apoptosis. Progesterone induces transcription factors FOXO1 and NR2F2, which coordinate upregulation of multiple antioxidant enzymes including glutathione peroxidases (GPX3/4), superoxide dismutase (SOD2), peroxiredoxins, and heme oxygenase-1. These enzymes work synergistically to detoxify reactive oxygen species, prevent lipid peroxidation, and inhibit ferroptosis during the high oxidative stress environment of implantation. The iron exporter SLC40A1 and lipid peroxidation inhibitor GPX4 play emerging roles in coordinating iron balance and cellular redox homeostasis during decidualization. Notably, endometriosis is characterized by impaired decidualization, progesterone resistance, and elevated reactive oxygen species, linking defective oxidative stress responses to this reproductive disorder.

title: Redox Homeostasis as a Therapeutic Target in Chronic Oxidative Diseases: Implications for Cancer Treatment
summary:
# Summary

This article examines redox homeostasis as a central therapeutic target in chronic oxidative diseases, with implications extending to endometriosis through ferroptosis and oxidative stress mechanisms. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) function as dual-natured molecules: at physiological low levels, they regulate essential processes including mitochondrial biogenesis, immune activation, and stem cell differentiation through controlled redox signaling, while at pathologically elevated but buffered levels, they sustain oncogenic signaling, metabolic rewiring, and therapeutic resistance. The KEAP1–NRF2 axis, along with transcription factors FOXO, HIFs, and NF-κB, coordinately regulate redox homeostasis and establish pathological cellular states; notably, NRF2-driven expression of SLC7A11 and GPX4 establishes ferroptosis resistance, a mechanism relevant to disease persistence. Persistent ROS generation from stressed tissues sustains NF-κB and HIF-dependent inflammatory and fibrotic programs, with redox-regulated markers including IL-6, TNF-α, and suPAR correlating with disease severity. The article emphasizes that indiscriminate ROS scavenging paradoxically disrupts essential redox signaling, suggesting that targeted modulation of redox-sensitive pathways rather than broad antioxidant approaches may represent more effective therapeutic strategies for chronic diseases.

title: Inducing Ferroptosis: Sensitization Strategy for Radiotherapy and Its Application
summary:
# Summary

This article examines ferroptosis as a sensitization mechanism to enhance radiotherapy efficacy in cancer treatment. Ionizing radiation induces ferroptosis through a multi-step pathway: it generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) that cause lipid peroxidation and simultaneously depletes glutathione (GSH) while upregulating ferroptosis-promoting factors like ACSL4. The ferroptotic process critically depends on iron accumulation and polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) peroxidation, which is catalyzed by ALOX enzymes and regulated by ACSL4 and LPCAT3. Conversely, ferroptosis is inhibited by protective mechanisms including GPX4 and SLC7A11 (which maintain GSH and reduce ROS), FSP1 (through CoQ10 regeneration), and the Nrf2 pathway; radiotherapy can overcome these defenses by downregulating these inhibitors. The article identifies multiple therapeutic strategies to enhance radiotherapy-mediated ferroptosis, including targeting glutamine metabolism, modulating tumor microenvironment through cancer-associated fibroblasts, and promoting ferroptosis in tumor-associated macrophages to enhance anti-tumor immunity.

title: Rescuing fertility: C-Phycocyanin prevents ovarian damage through NRF2-mediated ferroptosis pathways in polycystic ovary syndrome models
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that C-Phycocyanin (C-PC), a cyanobacteria-derived antioxidant protein, treats polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) by suppressing ferroptosis in ovarian granulosa cells through activation of the NRF2/xCT/GPX4 pathway. C-PC directly binds to and stabilizes NRF2, enabling its translocation to the nucleus where it enhances transcription of ferroptosis-protective genes xCT and GPX4, which inhibit iron-dependent lipid peroxidation driven by excessive reactive oxygen species. In both DHEA-induced PCOS mouse models and human granulosa cells, C-PC restored estrous cyclicity, reduced cystic follicles, normalized hormone levels (testosterone, estradiol, progesterone, luteinizing hormone), improved mitochondrial function, and enhanced antioxidant defense markers including glutathione and superoxide dismutase. Pharmacological and genetic NRF2 inhibition completely reversed C-PC's protective effects, confirming that NRF2 activation is essential for the compound's anti-ferroptotic and fertility-restoring properties. These findings establish ferroptosis as a key pathogenic mechanism in PCOS and identify NRF2-mediated ferroptosis inhibition as a promising therapeutic approach for restoring ovarian function and fertility.

title: The iron–ferroptosis axis in bone homeostasis: a potential complementary pathway for osteoporosis in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is clinically associated with increased osteoporosis risk through a multifaceted mechanism that extends beyond the well-established hypoestrogenic pathway. The disease's characteristic recurrent intrapelvic hemorrhage causes localized iron overload, which generates reactive oxygen species through the Fenton reaction and establishes a chronic pro-oxidative state that compromises bone homeostasis. This iron-rich inflammatory environment promotes ferroptosis—an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death—in bone cells, particularly osteocytes and osteoblasts, thereby impairing bone formation while simultaneously triggering osteoclast differentiation through elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines and RANKL secretion. Estrogen deficiency, whether from the disease itself or GnRH agonist treatment, further sensitizes bone cells to ferroptosis by downregulating critical antioxidant defenses including Nrf2, GPX4, and HO-1, while paradoxically promoting iron accumulation within bone tissue. The authors propose that endometriosis-derived inflammation and systemic iron dysregulation act as complementary pathways that amplify hormonally driven bone loss, potentially explaining why some patients develop severe osteoporosis. Notably, serum ferritin elevation correlates with low bone mineral density, and ferroptosis inhibitors like ferrostatin-1 and deferoxamine can reverse iron-induced bone cell death in vitro, suggesting novel therapeutic targets for this complication.

title: Targeting SLC6A8 suppresses tumor growth and enhances ferroptosis in hepatocellular carcinoma
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies SLC6A8, a creatine transporter, as a therapeutic target in hepatocellular carcinoma that promotes tumor progression through facilitating cellular creatine uptake and energy metabolism. SLC6A8 is significantly upregulated in HCC tissues and associated with poor prognosis, and its inhibition through knockdown or the inhibitor RGX-202 suppresses cell proliferation, migration, and tumor growth in vitro and in vivo. Mechanistically, SLC6A8 inhibition enhances ferroptosis sensitivity by increasing intracellular iron and reactive oxygen species while reducing expression of the ferroptosis-suppressor GPX4 and increasing the ferroptosis-driver ACSL4. Combined treatment with RGX-202 and the ferroptosis inducer RSL3 demonstrates enhanced anti-tumor efficacy compared to single agents, suggesting that targeting the creatine transport pathway synergizes with ferroptosis induction to overcome hepatocellular carcinoma progression. These findings establish SLC6A8 as a promising therapeutic target and highlight the potential of combining SLC6A8 inhibitors with ferroptosis inducers as a novel treatment strategy for HCC.

title: Acyl-CoA Synthetase Long-Chain Family Member 4 in Liver Injury: Multidimensional Regulation and Therapeutic Potential
summary: ACSL4 is a key enzyme that catalyzes the conjugation of long-chain fatty acids, particularly polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) like arachidonic acid, with coenzyme A to generate PUFA-CoA and PUFA-phosphatidylethanolamine (PUFA-PE), which are critical substrates for ferroptosis. ACSL4 is centrally involved in multiple liver pathologies including metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), liver fibrosis, hepatocellular carcinoma, and alcoholic liver disease, and is also implicated in ferroptosis regulation through lipid peroxidation when glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) dysfunction occurs alongside iron overload. ACSL4 expression is tightly regulated at multiple levels: transcriptionally by PPARδ and SREBP2; post-transcriptionally by microRNAs (miR-449, miR-424-5p, miR-4291) and competing circular RNAs (circLMO1, circSCN8A, circEXOC5); and post-translationally through ubiquitination-mediated degradation facilitated by CARM1 and lncRNA CBSLR, or stabilization by eIF3F. ACSL4 inhibition restores mitochondrial respiration and β-oxidation capacity, thereby ameliorating hepatic steatosis and offering therapeutic potential for liver disease. The protein localizes to multiple subcellular compartments including the endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria-associated ER membranes, peroxisomes, and plasma membrane, enabling its participation in diverse metabolic processes including mitochondrial β-oxidation, phosph

title: AMBRA1 activation alleviates zearalenone-induced swine testicular cell ferroptosis by facilitating mitophagy
journal: Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology
summary:
# Summary

Zearalenone, a prevalent mycotoxin, induces ferroptosis in testicular cells through mitochondrial dysfunction, excessive reactive oxygen species generation, and iron accumulation while simultaneously suppressing the AMBRA1-mediated mitophagy pathway. AMBRA1 is a critical autophagy regulator that enhances mitophagy both through PINK1-PARKIN-dependent mechanisms and independent pathways, functioning as a key cellular defense against ferroptosis by facilitating the clearance of damaged mitochondria. Zearalenone downregulates AMBRA1 expression, leading to impaired mitophagy and mitochondrial accumulation, which exacerbates oxidative stress and triggers ferroptosis in testicular cells. Notably, AMBRA1 overexpression effectively alleviates zearalenone-induced ferroptosis by restoring mitophagy capacity and maintaining redox homeostasis, while also regulating NRF2 signaling, a key ferroptosis suppressor. These findings demonstrate that AMBRA1-mediated mitophagy represents a crucial defense mechanism against zearalenone-induced testicular toxicity, offering potential therapeutic targets for mitigating reproductive damage from mycotoxin exposure.

title: The Bushen Huatan formula inhibits TP53-Mediated ferroptosis and modulating lipid metabolism in polycystic ovary syndrome abnormalities rat model
summary:
# Summary

The Bushen Huatan (BSHT) formula effectively treats polycystic ovary syndrome in rats by inhibiting TP53-mediated ferroptosis and restoring lipid metabolism balance. High-dose BSHT improved estrous cycle irregularities, reduced excessive testosterone and luteinizing hormone while increasing follicle-stimulating hormone, and ameliorated dyslipidemia by lowering cholesterol and low-density lipoprotein while elevating high-density lipoprotein. At the molecular level, BSHT suppressed oxidative stress and ferroptosis by decreasing malondialdehyde, reactive oxygen species, and lipid peroxides while enhancing the antioxidant glutathione and the ferroptosis-suppressor GPX4, with concurrent downregulation of the ferroptosis-promoter ACSL4. The therapeutic mechanism centers on BSHT's inhibition of the TP53 signaling pathway, which normally triggers ferroptosis and lipid dysmetabolism under the combined stress of hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance in ovarian granulosa cells. These findings demonstrate that BSHT's multimodal approach—combining endocrine rebalancing, lipid metabolism restoration, and ferroptosis suppression through TP53 inhibition—addresses the interconnected pathophysiology of PCOS-related reproductive dysfunction.

title: Nanomaterial-Induced Bacterial Ferroptosis-Like Death: A Novel Strategy to Counteract Antimicrobial Resistance
summary:
# Summary

Nanomaterial-induced bacterial ferroptosis-like death represents a promising strategy to combat antimicrobial resistance by exploiting iron-dependent cell death pathways. Nanomaterials act as iron reservoirs, promoting iron accumulation and influx within bacterial cells while simultaneously generating reactive oxygen species through chemodynamic, photodynamic, and sonodynamic mechanisms. These nanomaterials destroy the bacterial antioxidant defense system, leading to lipid peroxidation, glutathione depletion, and ultimately ferroptosis-like cell death characterized by iron accumulation and membrane damage. The approach synergistically activates host immune responses through macrophage polarization, enhancing bacterial clearance and tissue repair. Multiple nanomaterial platforms including gold nanoparticles, iron-loaded lipid nanoparticles, and Prussian blue nanoparticles have demonstrated efficacy against resistant pathogens such as methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus aureus*. This novel therapeutic strategy addresses the critical global health challenge of antimicrobial resistance, which causes approximately 7.7 million deaths annually worldwide.

title: Iron and Cancer
journal: Advances in experimental medicine and biology
summary:
# Summary: Iron and Cancer

Iron is essential for fundamental cellular processes including DNA synthesis, DNA repair, and cellular metabolism, making it critical for both normal and malignant cell function. Cancer cells exhibit enhanced iron demand compared to normal cells, achieving this through upregulation of iron import pathways, downregulation of iron export mechanisms, and alterations in iron storage and recycling proteins. Multiple regulatory systems control cancer cell iron metabolism, including hepcidin-mediated suppression of ferroportin (the primary iron exporter), IRP2-driven increases in transferrin receptor expression, and alternative uptake mechanisms like LCN2 and CD44-mediated pathways. The accumulated iron in cancer cells renders them susceptible to ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death, while simultaneously supporting mitochondrial iron-sulfur cluster biogenesis essential for oxidative phosphorylation and DNA repair. KRAS-mutated pancreatic cancers notably depend on ferritinophagy (ferritin autophagy) for iron availability, and disruption of this pathway synergizes with KRAS-MAPK inhibitors to suppress tumor growth. Iron gene expression signatures predict cancer prognosis across multiple malignancies, highlighting the therapeutic potential of targeting iron metabolism pathways as a strategy to inhibit tumor growth and overcome treatment resistance.

title: Multifunctional armored nanoemulsion of elemene combining ferroptosis induction and gut homeostasis restoration in colorectal cancer therapy
summary:
# Summary

This study describes LMP@EL-CNE, a polysaccharide-armored nanoemulsion combining elemene (EL), a natural compound from traditional Chinese medicine, with low-methoxyl pectin (LMP) coating for colorectal cancer therapy. The formulation induces ferroptosis in cancer cells by downregulating key ferroptosis regulators including GPX4, FTH1, and the SLC7A11/GPX4 axis, while simultaneously increasing mitochondrial dysfunction and reactive oxygen species accumulation. Beyond its cytotoxic effects, LMP@EL-CNE activates robust anti-tumor immunity by enhancing T-cell infiltration, elevating IFN-γ and TNF-α secretion, and reducing immune checkpoint markers (PD-1/Tim-3), thereby establishing an immunologically "hot" tumor microenvironment. The LMP coating provides dual pH and enzyme responsiveness, enabling colon-targeted delivery and subsequent restoration of gut homeostasis through intestinal barrier repair and modulation of commensal bacteria including Akkermansia and Lactobacillus. In orthotopic colorectal cancer models, LMP@EL-CNE demonstrated superior anti-tumor activity compared to free elemene, achieving multifunctional therapeutic benefits while avoiding the adverse effects associated with conventional multimodal drug combinations.

title: Metal ion-amplified phototherapy for tumors: Mechanisms, nanomaterial design, and synergistic strategies
summary:
# Summary

This review examines metal ion-amplified phototherapy as an emerging cancer treatment strategy that combines photodynamic and photothermal therapies with metal ion-dependent cell death pathways. Photodynamic therapy generates reactive oxygen species through photosensitizers to induce tumor cell death, while photothermal therapy converts light energy into heat; both approaches inhibit metastasis and activate local immunity without inducing drug resistance. The article emphasizes that multiple metal ions—including iron, copper, calcium, zinc, magnesium, and manganese—enhance treatment efficacy by triggering metal ion-dependent cell death mechanisms such as ferroptosis and cuproptosis. Ferroptosis, characterized by iron-dependent lipid peroxide accumulation through Fenton reactions, is regulated by the GPX4 enzyme and glutathione system, while cuproptosis involves copper-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and reactive oxygen species generation. Calcium ions amplify phototherapy-induced cell death by triggering mitochondrial permeability transition and cytochrome c release, demonstrating synergistic interactions between phototherapy and metal-mediated oxidative stress pathways. This integrated approach overcomes key limitations such as tumor hypoxia-induced phototherapy resistance by leveraging multiple metal ions to enhance selective cancer cell elimination.

title: Liproxstatin-1 Attenuates Retinal Ischemia–Reperfusion Injury by Suppressing EGR1-Mediated Ferroptosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that liproxstatin-1 (Lip-1) protects against retinal ischemia–reperfusion injury by suppressing ferroptosis through inhibition of the EGR1–p53–xCT signaling axis. Retinal ischemia–reperfusion injury triggers ferroptosis, a form of iron-dependent cell death characterized by glutathione depletion, GPX4 inactivation, and lipid peroxidation, leading to retinal ganglion cell death and vision loss. Liproxstatin-1 attenuates this injury by downregulating EGR1, which normally activates pro-ferroptotic gene programs and inflammatory cascades, thereby restoring xCT expression and redox homeostasis. Treatment with liproxstatin-1 preserves retinal architecture, restores visual function, and prevents multiple forms of neuronal cell death including retinal ganglion cells, amacrine cells, and axonal truncation in bipolar cells in a dose-dependent manner. This work establishes ferroptosis as a key mechanism of ischemic retinal injury and identifies the EGR1–p53–xCT axis as a targetable pathway for neuroprotection, with potential therapeutic implications for ischemic retinal diseases.

title: Therapeutic rewiring of ceRNA networks: a computational pipeline for drug repurposing in acute kidney injury via circRNA–miRNA–mRNA axis disruption
summary:
# Summary

This article presents a computational framework for drug repurposing in acute kidney injury (AKI) by therapeutically rewiring competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) networks, particularly the circRNA–miRNA–mRNA regulatory axes. AKI is a clinically severe condition caused by ischemia–reperfusion injury, sepsis, or nephrotoxins like cisplatin, and carries substantial mortality risk alongside persistent chronic kidney disease progression. The ceRNA model shows that circular RNAs act as molecular sponges to titrate microRNA availability, thereby modulating protein-coding gene networks; exemplar circRNAs such as circHIPK3 are implicated in sepsis-AKI and cisplatin-induced injury, with knockdown reducing oxidative stress and apoptosis. The authors propose two complementary therapeutic paradigms: disrupting pathogenic circRNAs to release microRNA repression or engineering circRNA sponges to sequester injurious miRNAs that drive apoptosis (miR-16-5p via BCL2), oxidative stress (miR-144-3p), and fibrosis (miR-150-5p). The practical pipeline integrates differential expression analysis, correlation studies, and validated target prediction with biochemical assays (AGO-CLIP, PAR-CLIP) and functional perturbations (circRNA overexpression/knockdown, miRNA mimics/antagomirs) to nominate therapeutic triplets, enabling rapid translation of mechanistic insights into repurposed drugs.

title: Role of Copper Homeostasis and Cuproptosis in Cardiovascular Disease: Molecular Insights and Metabolic Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Copper homeostasis critically regulates cardiovascular health through multiple interconnected mechanisms, with dysregulation contributing to major cardiac pathologies including heart failure, cardiomyopathy, atherosclerosis, and myocardial ischemia-reperfusion injury. Copper functions as an essential cofactor for vital metabolic enzymes including cytochrome c oxidase, superoxide dismutase, and lysyl oxidase, supporting mitochondrial respiration, antioxidant defense, and structural integrity of cardiac tissue. Cuproptosis, a copper-dependent form of regulated cell death triggered by copper binding to lipoylated enzymes in the mitochondrial tricarboxylic acid cycle, represents a distinct mechanism driving cardiovascular pathology and inducing proteotoxic stress. Copper overload promotes excessive reactive oxygen species generation through Fenton-like reactions, impairs antioxidant defenses, disrupts mitochondrial dynamics, and activates pro-fibrotic signaling pathways including transforming growth factor-beta, collectively driving pathological cardiac remodeling and dysfunction. Both copper excess and deficiency prove deleterious; elevated serum copper levels associate with cardiometabolic risk factors including dyslipidemia, type 2 diabetes, and obesity, and predict cardiovascular disease risk. Understanding copper-dependent cell death pathways and metabolic dysregulation offers potential therapeutic targets for managing copper-related cardiovascular injury and improving cardiac outcomes.

title: Emodin triggers cuproptosis to suppress hepatocellular carcinoma via SLC7A11/FDX1 axis
summary: Emodin, a natural anthraquinone compound, suppresses hepatocellular carcinoma by triggering cuproptosis through modulation of the SLC7A11/FDX1 axis. The mechanism involves emodin binding to and upregulating ferredoxin 1 (FDX1) while simultaneously downregulating SLC7A11 and GPX4, two key regulators of glutathione homeostasis that normally protect cells from copper toxicity. This dual action disrupts cellular antioxidant defenses and facilitates copper accumulation, which FDX1 converts to its toxic form to initiate cuproptosis. FDX1 knockdown experiments confirmed its essential role, as cells lacking FDX1 showed significantly reduced copper accumulation, restored glutathione levels, and rescued viability despite emodin treatment. In vivo studies demonstrated that emodin administration reduced tumor volumes in a dose-dependent manner and correlated with increased FDX1 expression in necrotic tumor areas. These findings establish cuproptosis as a previously uncharacterized anti-tumor mechanism of emodin and suggest its therapeutic potential for HCC treatment.

title: Zuogui Wan alleviated radiation-induced premature ovarian failure and ferroptosis via miR-21-5p/DNMT3b
summary:
# Summary

Gamma radiation induces premature ovarian failure (POF) through ferroptosis, a form of iron-dependent cell death that damages oocytes and granulosa cells while depleting protective antioxidants like glutathione (GSH) and glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4). The traditional Chinese medicine Zuogui Wan alleviates radiation-induced POF and ferroptosis by upregulating miR-21-5p, which suppresses its target DNMT3b and activates the AKT/mTOR signaling pathway to restore antioxidant defenses and reduce iron accumulation. This miR-21-5p/DNMT3b axis is essential for the therapeutic effect, as inhibiting miR-21-5p or overexpressing DNMT3b completely abolishes Zuogui Wan's protective effects. Treatment with Zuogui Wan restores serum hormone levels (estradiol and AMH), reduces lipid peroxidation and reactive oxygen species, and improves follicle counts and ovarian morphology in radiation-damaged ovaries. These findings identify ferroptosis as a key mechanism in radiation-induced POF and establish the miR-21-5p/DNMT3b axis as a promising therapeutic target for protecting ovarian function during cancer treatment.

title: Recent progress in understanding ferroptosis mechanisms in infectious diseases
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and reactive oxygen species accumulation through Fenton reactions involving ferrous iron. The process is tightly regulated by multiple pathways, including the SLC7A11-GPX4 axis, which maintains cellular redox balance through glutathione; classic inducers like Erastin and RSL3 trigger ferroptosis by inhibiting this pathway. Conversely, several anti-ferroptotic mechanisms exist, including the FSP1-CoQH2 and GCH1-BH4 pathways, which degrade lipid peroxides and modulate phospholipid composition respectively. Multiple pathogens including SARS-CoV-2, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa manipulate ferroptosis to evade immunity and establish infection by dysregulating iron metabolism and ROS levels. Beyond infectious disease, ferroptosis contributes to pathogenesis in cancer, neurodegenerative diseases, and stroke, making it a potential therapeutic target through drugs like sorafenib and ferroptosis-inducing compounds. Understanding ferroptosis regulation offers novel strategies for treating both infectious and non-infectious diseases by either promoting ferroptosis in cancer cells or inhibiting it to protect neurons and other vital tissues.

title: Protective effects of SESN2 on Erastin-induced ferroptosis in renal tubular cells and acute kidney injury
journal: Journal of Central South University Medical Sciences
summary: This study demonstrates that sestrin2 (SESN2) provides substantial protection against Erastin-induced ferroptosis and acute kidney injury through activation of the Nrf2 signaling pathway. Erastin triggers ferroptosis in renal tubular cells by inhibiting the System Xc cystine transporter, blocking glutathione (GSH) synthesis and leading to elevated reactive oxygen species, lipid peroxidation, and iron accumulation. SESN2 counteracts these pathological processes by activating Nrf2, which upregulates the ferroptosis-inhibitory proteins GPX4 and FSP1—which respectively neutralize phospholipid hydroperoxides and regenerate reduced coenzyme Q10. In both cultured HK-2 renal tubular cells and ICR mice with Erastin-induced acute kidney injury, SESN2 overexpression significantly reduced markers of cellular damage (LDH, ROS, MDA, Fe²⁺) and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) while restoring GSH levels and reducing apoptosis. Functionally, SESN2 treatment normalized serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels in injured mice, indicating recovery of renal function. These findings establish SESN2 as a key endogenous protective mechanism against ferroptosis-mediated acute kidney injury and suggest the Nrf2 pathway as a viable therapeutic target.
